title: Language Without Sound
subtitle: Brain networks, pathway expansion, literacy, and the case for SignWriting infrastructure
summary: Presentation 1 in The Science of Sutton SignWriting series.
synopsis: Language is not tied to sound. Spoken, signed, tactile, and written pathways together show that human language is not reducible to one privileged sensory channel. Sutton SignWriting provides a durable written pathway for signed language and opens the door to literacy, research, and shared infrastructure.
cover_image_alt: Cover image for Language Without Sound in The Science of Sutton SignWriting series.

publication_plan.doi_strategy: publish as distinct citable artifacts connected by series identity and related identifiers
publication_plan.zenodo_record_title_suggestion: Language Without Sound: Brain Networks, Pathway Expansion, Literacy, and the Case for SignWriting Infrastructure
publication_plan.citation_stub: Slevinski, S. (2026). Language Without Sound: Brain Networks, Pathway Expansion, Literacy, and the Case for SignWriting Infrastructure. The Science of Sutton SignWriting.

related_identifiers.note: Do not reuse the Formal SignWriting DOI. Link this work as a related scientific framing artifact.

slides.title: Language in the Brain
slides.summary: Language is not sound. It is a brain system.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for language networks in the brain.

image_prompts.concrete: Scientific brain illustration with Broca's area and Wernicke's area labeled, arrows from ear and eyes converging on a common language network, clean academic slide graphic, white background
image_prompts.abstract: Glowing brain network with multiple streams of input merging into one center of meaning, no text, no sound waves, elegant scientific abstraction

bullets: Language is a distributed network
Input routes differ
Core language systems converge
Speech, sign, and touch are pathways
slides.speech:
We often talk as if language lives in the ear and the mouth. That is understandable, because speech is the most familiar case. But it is not the whole case. Language is something the brain builds. Different sensory routes can deliver it, and different motor systems can express it, but the organizing work of structure and meaning happens in shared language networks.

That shift matters for everything that follows. If language is a brain system rather than a sound system, then speech is one pathway, not the definition. Sign is another pathway. Touch can support language and literacy as well. Once we start there, the rest of the argument becomes much easier to see.
slides.notes:
Main point:
- language is a brain system, not a sound system
- speech, sign, touch, and written expression are pathways
- move through the diagram from input to shared network to output

Keep the emphasis on convergence rather than competition.

Transition:
- Start with the pathway everyone already accepts: spoken language.

slides.title: Spoken Language Pathway (Established Science)
slides.summary: Spoken language begins with sensory preprocessing before meaning.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for the spoken language pathway.

image_prompts.concrete: Educational neuroscience diagram of spoken language processing, ear receiving sound, auditory cortex sorting speech from noise, language centers activating speech production, clear labels, realistic but simplified
image_prompts.abstract: Sound waves transforming into organized neural patterns and then into spoken expression, luminous scientific concept art

bullets: Speech uses a sensory route into language
Auditory preprocessing comes before meaning
Production is a second pathway out
slides.speech:
Spoken language is the case everyone already accepts, and it helps because it shows the pathway clearly. Sound arrives at the ear, but at that point it is only acoustic signal. The brain has to separate speech from noise, organize patterns, and connect those patterns to meaning. Only then do we get language as language.

The output side matters too. Once a thought is planned linguistically, production systems recruit breath, articulation, and the vocal tract. So even here, speech is not the same thing as language. It is one pathway into language and one pathway out of it. That is why spoken language is a model for the larger argument, not an exception to it.
slides.notes:
Main point:
- speech is the familiar example of a language pathway
- raw sound is not yet language
- production is a separate route back out

Land the distinction between acoustic signal and meaning.

Transition:
- If speech has a pathway, sign language should have one too.

slides.title: Visual Language Pathway (Sign Language)
slides.summary: Sign language shows that language does not require sound.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for the visual language pathway.

image_prompts.concrete: Person signing with overlays showing handshape, movement arrows, facial expression, and visual cortex leading into language centers, clean scientific infographic
image_prompts.abstract: Streams of light shaped like hands and facial patterns entering a brain through the eyes, transforming into meaning, elegant nonverbal language concept

bullets: Sign language enters through vision
Multiple visual features must be integrated
Language emerges after visual analysis
This is a full language pathway
slides.speech:
Sign language shows the same architecture using a different route. The eyes receive a dense linguistic signal made of handshape, movement, location, facial expression, and spatial relationships. Those are not decorations around language. They are part of the language itself. Visual systems organize that signal, and the brain maps it into structure and meaning.

That is why sign language matters so much for this discussion. It is not speech with the sound removed, and it is not gesture waiting to be interpreted. It is language entering through vision. Once that is clear, the idea that language is inherently auditory starts to collapse. The route changes. The status as language does not.
slides.notes:
Main point:
- sign language enters through vision and remains fully linguistic
- handshape, movement, facial expression, and space are part of the signal
- the route changes, not the status as language

Mirror the spoken pathway so the analogy feels obvious.

Transition:
- Once vision can carry language, touch becomes relevant too.
slides.mermaid:
flowchart TD
  subgraph VisualSignal[Visual Linguistic Signal]
    direction LR
    A[Handshape]
    B[Movement]
    C[Facial Expression]
    D[Location and Space]
  end

  A --> E[Visual Pattern Analysis]
  B --> E
  C --> E
  D --> E
  E --> F[Integrated Sign Perception]
  F --> G[Language Interpretation]
  G --> H[Meaning and Grammar]
  H --> I[Signing Plan]
  I --> J[Hands, Face, Body]

  classDef visual fill:#eff6ff,color:black,stroke:#2563eb,stroke-width:2px;
  classDef process fill:#f8fafc,color:black,stroke:#64748b,stroke-width:2px;

slides.title: Tactile Language and Tactile Literacy
slides.summary: Braille and tactile signing show that language and literacy can also travel through touch.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for tactile language and tactile literacy.

image_prompts.concrete: Scientific diagram showing Braille reading through the fingers and tactile signing through the hands, with somatosensory and occipital reading regions connected to language centers, clean academic visual
image_prompts.abstract: Touch-based pathways flowing from hands into a brain and converging on language networks, elegant scientific concept art

bullets: Braille and tactile signing are not the same
One is mainly literacy, one is language access
Both matter for the larger argument
Touch can participate in language pathways
slides.speech:
Touch broadens the argument, but only if we keep the categories straight. Braille and tactile signing are both tactile, but they are not doing the same job. Braille is usually a literacy pathway. It gives reading and writing through touch. Tactile signing is a language-access pathway. It lets language itself be received and expressed through touch.

That distinction is not a complication. It is a clarification. Braille shows that literacy does not require vision. Tactile signing shows that language access does not require audition or even vision. Together they make the same larger point from different angles: neither language nor literacy belongs to one privileged sensory route.
slides.notes:
Main point:
- keep Braille and tactile signing distinct
- Braille is usually literacy through touch
- tactile signing is language access through touch

Use the distinction to strengthen the argument rather than complicate it.

Transition:
- The next question is not which channel is normal, but which input the learner can actually use.
slides.mermaid:
flowchart LR
  A[Touch] --> B[Somatosensory Pattern Analysis]

  subgraph Literacy[Tactile Literacy Route]
    C[Braille]
    D[Tactile Symbols]
    E[Reading and Meaning]
  end

  subgraph Language[Tactile Language Route]
    F[Tactile Signing]
    G[Tactile Linguistic Input]
    H[Language Access and Conversation]
  end

  B --> D --> E
  B --> G --> H
  C --> D
  F --> G

  classDef touch fill:#eff6ff,color:black,stroke:#2563eb,stroke-width:2px;
  classDef literacy fill:#fefce8,color:black,stroke:#ca8a04,stroke-width:2px;
  classDef language fill:#f5f3ff,color:black,stroke:#7c3aed,stroke-width:3px;
  class A,B touch;
  class C,D,E literacy;
  class F,G,H language;

slides.title: Brain Plasticity and Accessible Input
slides.summary: Early development organizes language through accessible input, not through a preferred sensory channel.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for brain plasticity and accessible input.

image_prompts.concrete: Brain scan style illustration showing neural reorganization around accessible spoken, signed, and tactile input, academic and respectful, high contrast neuroscience visual
image_prompts.abstract: Neural pathways rewiring and lighting up in multiple directions, a brain adapting to accessible language, scientific but poetic

bullets: Early development organizes language pathways
Cross-modal plasticity supports integration
Access matters more than sensory loyalty
slides.speech:
Now we get to development. The first major event in a child's development is not reading. It is the formation of a usable language system. That happens when language is accessible, repeated, and socially meaningful. The brain organizes around the input it can actually use.

That is where plasticity matters. In spoken development, audition can carry the load. In signed development, vision can carry the load. In some tactile contexts, touch can help carry it as well. The important point is not which channel wins. The important point is that early language pathways form and integrate when accessible language reaches the brain. Literacy comes later and builds on that foundation.
slides.notes:
Main point:
- early development is about building language pathways
- accessible input matters more than loyalty to one channel
- literacy comes later and builds on this base

Use plasticity as the scientific reason the argument works.

Transition:
- Once access becomes the issue, availability and accessibility have to be separated.

slides.title: Language Available vs Language Accessible
slides.summary: The central issue is not only what language exists around the child, but what language the child can actually perceive.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for language available versus language accessible.

image_prompts.concrete: Child development infographic comparing accessible language input with language that is present but only partially perceivable, respectful educational style
image_prompts.abstract: Two branching pathways, one connected and bright, one fragmented and incomplete, representing access versus mere availability

bullets: Language nearby is not the same as language perceived
Early access matters
Partial access is not full access
Deprivation risk is real
slides.speech:
This is the ethical center of the talk. Language can be present in a child's environment without actually being available to that child as language. A deaf child surrounded by speech but without accessible sign is not simply taking a slower version of the same path. The conditions are different. The same is true whenever tactile access is needed and not available.

So the real question is not what language is nearby. The real question is what language the learner can actually perceive, use, and build on. That distinction matters because early access shapes the language foundation itself. Availability is not access, and partial access is not the same as a stable language pathway.
slides.notes:
Main point:
- language in the environment is not enough by itself
- the learner has to be able to perceive and use it
- partial access is not the same as stable access

This is the place to slow down and let the ethical weight show.

Transition:
- With that distinction in place, the case comparisons become much clearer.

slides.title: Reading Adds Durable Pathways
slides.summary: Literacy adds durable symbolic input and output to an already linguistic brain.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for reading and the brain.

image_prompts.concrete: Brain illustration highlighting reading regions with both printed text entering through the eyes and Braille entering through the fingers, professional educational style
image_prompts.abstract: Written symbols and tactile symbols dissolving into neural patterns and then into meaning

bullets: Language comes first
Reading and writing come later
Print and Braille take different routes
Both create durable symbol-to-meaning pathways
slides.speech:
Once language is in place, literacy adds a second layer. It does not create language from nothing. It adds durable symbolic input and output to a brain that is already linguistic. Print does this through vision. Braille does it through touch. The routes differ, but the achievement is the same: symbols become stable, revisitable, and shareable carriers of language.

That is why literacy matters so much here. It changes what can be reread, revised, compared, searched, taught, and studied. It turns fleeting language into durable text. And once we accept that literacy can travel through more than one route, the next question becomes unavoidable: what is the corresponding written pathway for signed language itself?
slides.notes:
Main point:
- language develops first
- literacy adds durable symbolic input and durable symbolic output
- print and Braille differ in route, but they do the same kind of work

Emphasize that literacy is not mere storage. It changes what people can do with language over time.

Transition:
- Use that shared function to set up the cases.

slides.title: Comparative Cases
slides.summary: A small set of cases clarifies where language pathways end and literacy pathways begin.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for comparative language and literacy cases.

image_prompts.concrete: Structured academic slide showing comparative pathway cases for hearing speech with print, deaf early sign, blind Braille, deaf sign with SignWriting, and a contrast case of language present but inaccessible, clean and readable
image_prompts.abstract: A branching map of language and literacy pathways across hearing, deaf, blind, and signed writing contexts, converging on shared principles

bullets: Hearing plus print: language first, print later
Deaf plus early sign: language without sound
Blind plus Braille: literacy without vision
Deaf plus sign plus SignWriting: written pathways inside signed language
Contrast case: availability is not access
slides.speech:
The easiest way to keep the model clear is to compare a few strategic cases. Hearing children show the familiar pattern: spoken language develops first, and print literacy comes later. Deaf children with early sign show that language itself does not depend on sound. Blind children with Braille show that literacy does not depend on vision. And the case that matters most here is signed language with SignWriting, where the language gains readable and writable pathways within itself.

Then keep one contrast case in view. Language can be socially present and still not be sufficiently accessible to the learner. That is where development becomes unstable, and later literacy cannot simply repair the missing foundation. Together, these comparisons show that the key variables are not sound versus silence or sight versus touch. The key variables are access, durability, and whether the pathway belongs to the language itself.
slides.notes:
Main point:
- use strategic contrasts, not an exhaustive catalog
- hearing plus print gives the familiar sequence
- early sign and Braille show route flexibility
- SignWriting is the case where signed language gains its own written pathway
- the contrast case keeps deprivation risk visible

Move briskly through the first three cases and give the SignWriting case the most emphasis.

Transition:
- Those contrasts make the structural gap easier to name.
slides.mermaid:
flowchart TD
  A[Strategic Cases] --> B[Hearing plus Speech plus Print]
  A --> C[Deaf plus Early Sign]
  A --> D[Blind plus Speech plus Braille]
  A --> E[Deaf plus Early Sign plus SignWriting]
  A --> F[Language Present but Not Fully Accessible]

  B --> G[Language develops first; print adds literacy]
  C --> H[Language does not require sound]
  D --> I[Literacy does not require vision]
  E --> J[Signed language can gain direct written pathways]
  F --> K[Availability is not access]

  G --> L[Pathways explain the differences]
  H --> L
  I --> L
  J --> L
  K --> L

  classDef case fill:#eff6ff,color:black,stroke:#2563eb,stroke-width:2px;
  classDef finding fill:#fefce8,color:black,stroke:#ca8a04,stroke-width:2px;
  classDef outcome fill:#f5f3ff,color:black,stroke:#7c3aed,stroke-width:3px;
  class A,B,C,D,E,F case;
  class G,H,I,J,K finding;
  class L outcome;

slides.title: The Structural Gap and Sutton SignWriting
slides.summary: The missing issue is not language itself, but durable native-facing written pathways.

image_prompts.concrete: Page of Sutton SignWriting symbols with examples of handshape, movement, and facial expression, clean real-world educational layout, international and modern
image_prompts.abstract: Hand movements turning into structured written symbols on a page, visual language crystallizing into literacy

bullets: Video preserves performance
Research transcription supports specialist analysis
Neither replaces everyday reading and writing
SignWriting adds the missing written layer
slides.speech:
This is the missing layer. Signed languages are full languages. The gap is not linguistic legitimacy. The gap is that signed languages have often lacked equally mature written pathways for everyday reading, writing, reuse, and structured work. In practice, that leaves too much of the ecosystem dependent on video and specialist transcription.

Both of those are valuable. Video preserves performance. Transcription supports specialist analysis. But neither one is the same thing as durable, readable, writable text inside the language itself. That difference matters. Without writable pathways, signed languages remain at a systemic disadvantage in education, research, and technology, not because of any weakness in the language, but because of missing infrastructure. SignWriting addresses that gap by adding a direct written layer for signed language.
slides.notes:
Main point:
- the gap is not whether signed languages are real languages
- the gap is the missing written layer
- video and transcription are useful, but they do different jobs from writing
- SignWriting adds readable and writable sign text inside the language itself

Use the phrase "missing layer" deliberately here. This is the resolution moment of the argument.

Transition:
- Once that written layer exists, the next step is infrastructure.

slides.title: From Writing System to Infrastructure
slides.summary: Durable written sign lets language function as text across research, education, and technology.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for SignWriting as scientific infrastructure.

image_prompts.concrete: Research lab scene combining SignWriting text, eye-tracking setup, brain imaging visuals, and language modeling data on screens, realistic academic environment
image_prompts.abstract: A network of signs, eyes, brain scans, and data streams connected into one research ecosystem

bullets: Writable sign creates durable text
Durable text supports corpora, lexicons, and annotation
Shared text supports experiments, datasets, and tools
That is how a writing system becomes infrastructure
slides.speech:
Once a language has durable written pathways, new forms of work become much more feasible. Text can be collected into corpora. Corpora can support annotation, lexicons, and reusable datasets. Those in turn support experiments, teaching materials, software, and collaborative workflows. The point is not that writing magically solves everything. The point is that writing changes the unit of work. Instead of depending only on time-based media, researchers and communities can work with structured text.

That is why the argument does not stop at orthography. With writable sign, the pieces that are now fragmented can begin to cohere: standards, tools, educational materials, computational pipelines, and repeatable research protocols. This is the bridge from writing system to infrastructure.
slides.notes:
Main point:
- durable text changes what kinds of work become practical
- corpora, lexicons, annotation, and datasets grow from writable text
- infrastructure is what happens when those pieces become shared and repeatable

Give one or two concrete examples: literacy studies, eye-tracking stimuli, searchable corpora, or structured NLP data.

Transition:
- After the bridge, separate what is already established from what still needs research.

slides.title: Established Science vs Research Opportunity
slides.summary: Established science makes the topic credible; open questions make it worth building.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for established science versus research opportunity.

image_prompts.concrete: Academic infographic split into established findings and open research questions, clear modern science presentation style
image_prompts.abstract: A completed structure beside an unfinished blueprint, representing known science and new discovery

bullets: Established findings carry the foundation
Open questions define the research agenda
Rigor comes from keeping those levels separate
slides.speech:
I want to be disciplined about what can be claimed strongly and what should remain an open question. Some parts of this argument are already well established. Human language is not reducible to sound. Sign languages recruit core language networks. Plasticity supports visual and tactile routes. Those findings are strong enough to justify taking the topic seriously.

The next layer is the research agenda. How does the brain read visual-spatial writing? How do tactile and visual literacy pathways compare? What changes when signers gain direct written access to their own language? Which written supports help which learners most? Keeping the foundation separate from the frontier does not weaken the case. It is what makes the case scientifically credible.
slides.notes:
Main point:
- separate strong foundations from genuine open questions
- established science gives the project legitimacy
- open questions explain why building infrastructure is worthwhile now

This slide is where rigor becomes persuasive. Avoid sounding defensive; sound precise.

Transition:
- If the scientific case is real, continuity becomes the practical issue.

slides.title: Infrastructure Need
slides.summary: The ecosystem already exists; continuity, stewardship, and institutional support are the weak points.

image_prompts.concrete: Global SignWriting infrastructure concept with websites, databases, education use, and a visible weak point showing fragile support, professional academic advocacy visual
image_prompts.abstract: A widely used bridge supported by too few pillars, suggesting the need for stronger foundations

bullets: Real users, tools, standards, and history already exist
Support is still fragile
Fragility limits continuity, maintenance, and growth
Institutional backing changes the trajectory
slides.speech:
By this point, the issue should no longer look hypothetical. SignWriting already has real users, real software, real standards work, and a real scholarly history. The weakness is not existence. The weakness is continuity.

When support is too thin, everything becomes harder: maintenance, documentation, succession, collaboration, funding, and reliability. That matters for daily users, and it matters for science. If we want durable literacy work, durable datasets, and durable research programs, the system needs more than interest. It needs an institutional home, collaborators, and steady technical stewardship.
slides.notes:
Main point:
- SignWriting is already real in use and in practice
- the weak point is continuity, not legitimacy
- stewardship and institutional support change maintenance, succession, and reliability

Keep this grounded. The force comes from practicality, not exaggeration.

Transition:
- Once continuity is visible as the bottleneck, the upside of shared infrastructure becomes clearer.

slides.title: Strategic Opportunity
slides.summary: One shared SignWriting infrastructure can serve many disciplines at once.

image_prompts.concrete: Interdisciplinary research network diagram featuring linguists, neuroscientists, AI researchers, educators, and Deaf community users connected through SignWriting platforms
image_prompts.abstract: A central hub radiating connections to science, education, accessibility, and technology, bright and forward-looking

bullets: Shared text, tools, corpora, and datasets
One asset base, many disciplines
Scientific, educational, and social value align here
slides.speech:
This is where the opportunity becomes larger than any single field. The same SignWriting infrastructure can support readable text, standards work, corpora, datasets, experiments, and shared tools. Once those pieces exist, they do not belong to only one discipline. They support linguistics and literacy, education and accessibility, neuroscience, AI, and international collaboration at the same time.

That convergence is unusual. Scientific value, educational value, and social value often live in separate projects. Here they can grow from the same asset base. That is why the strategic opportunity is so strong. Building one shared layer can unlock many kinds of work at once.
slides.notes:
Main point:
- one shared asset base can support many fields at the same time
- readable text, tools, corpora, datasets, and experiments reinforce one another
- the unusual feature here is convergence: many kinds of value come from the same infrastructure

Walk from the center to the enabling layers, then to the fields, then to the combined value.

Transition:
- Close by restating the argument in its simplest form.

slides.title: Conclusion / Contact / Collaboration
slides.summary: Language, literacy, and writable sign lead to a concrete collaborative agenda.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for the conclusion and collaboration close.

image_prompts.concrete: Closing slide image showing brain, signing hands, and written SignWriting symbols connected in one clean composition, polished academic keynote style
image_prompts.abstract: A final image of meaning emerging from silence: brain networks, hands, and written symbols united in light, elegant and hopeful

bullets: Language does not depend on sound
Literacy does not depend on speech or vision
Literacy does depend on a writable system
SignWriting addresses that gap
slides.speech:
So the argument ends in a very simple sequence. Language does not depend on sound. Literacy does not depend on speech or vision. But literacy does depend on having a writable system. Spoken languages have writing. Blind readers have Braille. Signed languages have full linguistic structure, but they have often lacked equally mature written pathways. SignWriting addresses that gap.

Once signed language can function as durable text, the question is no longer whether the idea is possible. The question becomes how to build and sustain the infrastructure that lets it work at scale: in research, in teaching, in technology, and in everyday use. That is the collaboration I am looking for.
slides.notes:
Main point:
- close with the strongest short-form version of the argument
- use the spoken language / Braille / signed language sequence slowly and clearly
- end on collaboration as the practical next step

Pause after "But literacy does depend on having a writable system." Then let the final sentence narrow into the ask for collaborators and institutional partners.
